coredata.dc:description: Low fluorescence under visible light excitation and catalytic activity limit many applications of graphene quantum dots in optical detection, biosensing, catalysis and biomedical. The paper reports design and synthesis of histidine, serine and folic acid-functionalized and boron and iron-doped graphene quantum dot (Fe/B-GQD-HSF). The Fe/B-GQD-HSF shows excellent fluorescence behavior and peroxidase-like activity. Excitation of 330 nm ultraviolet light produces the strongest blue fluorescence and excitation of 480 nm visible light produces the strongest yellow fluorescence. The specific activity reaches 92.67 U g−1, which is higher than that of other graphene quantum dots. The Fe/B-GQD-HSF can catalyze oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine with H2O2 to form blue compound. Based on this, it was used for colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2. The absorbance at 652 nm linearly increases with the increase of H2O2 concentration between 0.5 and 100 μM with detection limit of 0.43 μM. The fluorescence signal linearly decreases with the increase of H2O2 concentration between 0.05 and 100 μM with detection limit of 0.035 μM. The analytical method has been satisfactorily applied in detection of H2O2 in food. The study also paves one way for design and synthesis of functional graphene quantum dots with ideal fluorescence behavior and catalytic activity.

Total XPS spectrum (A), high-resolution XPS spectra of B1s (B), C1s (C), N1s (D), O1s (E) and Fe2p (F) of Fe/B-GQD-HSF.

Three-dimensional fluorescence mapping of Fe/B-GQD-HSF (A), plots of excitation wavelength vs. peak fluorescence intensity (Fp) and maximum emission wavelength (λem) (B), CIE coordinate excited within a wide wavenumber range of 460–620 nm (C) and Fp values at excitation of 330 nm ultraviolet light and 480 nm visible light for different irradiation times with ultraviolet lamp of 100 W of Fe/B-GQD-HSF. Other conditions: Fe/B-GQD-HSF: 0.05 mgmL−1, pH value: the RB of pH 7.4, and the temperature: 25 °C.

The absorption spectra (A) of TMB oxidation catalyzed by Fe/B-GQD-HSF, B-GQD-HSF, GQD-HSF, GQD-HSF, Fe-GQD and GQD at the incubation time of 30 min, relationship curves of the reaction rate (V) with H2O2 concentration (B) and reciprocal of H2O2 concentration (C), relationship curves of the reaction rate (V) with TMB concentration (D) and reciprocal of TMB concentration (E), relationship curves of the absorbances at 652 nm with incubation time in the presence of 0.0001, 0.00012, 0.00014, 0.00016, 0.00018 and 0.0002 mg Fe/B-GQD-HSF, and relationship curve of the peroxidase-like activity (U mg−1) with the mass of Fe/B-GQD-HSF. The error bar represents the standard deviation of five measurements.

Absorption spectra (A) of the different reaction systems at the incubation time of 30 min, including Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB+H2O2, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+H2O2, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB, TMB+H2O2, TMB and Fe/B-GQD-HSF, plots of absorbance at 652 nm vs. Fe/B-GQD-HSF concentration (B), pH (C) and incubation time (D), absorption spectra and optical photographs (Insert) of the reaction system in the presence of 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 30, 50, 80, 100 and 300 μM H2O2 at the incubation time of 30 min (E), and relationship curve of absorbance at 652 nm with the H2O2 concentration (F). The error bar represents the standard deviation of five measurements.

Fluorescence spectra of TMB, Fe/B-GQD-HSF, TMB+H2O2, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+H2O2, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB and Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB+H2O2 reaction systems under the excitations of 330 nm ultraviolet light (A) and 480 nm visible light (B), fluorescence spectra of Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB+H2O2 reaction system at the excitations of 330 nm ultraviolet light (C) and 480 nm visible light (D) in the presence of 0.05, 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 30, 50, 80, 100, 300, 500, 800 and 1000 μM H2O2, relationship curve of the peak fluorescence values at the excitations of 330 nm ultraviolet light and 480 nm visible light (F330/F480) with the H2O2 concentration (E), and ratio (F/F0) of peak fluorescence intensity before (F0) and after added interference specie (F). The error bar represents the standard deviation of five measurements.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The paper report synthesis of histidine, serine and folic acid-functionalized and boron and iron-doped graphene quantum dot by hydrothermal treatment. The resulting graphene quantum dot offers excellent fluorescence property and high peroxidase-like activity.

Low fluorescence under visible light excitation and catalytic activity limit many applications of graphene quantum dots in optical detection, biosensing, catalysis and biomedical. The paper reports design and synthesis of histidine, serine and folic acid-functionalized and boron and iron-doped graphene quantum dot (Fe/B-GQD-HSF). The Fe/B-GQD-HSF shows excellent fluorescence behavior and peroxidase-like activity. Excitation of 330 nm ultraviolet light produces the strongest blue fluorescence and excitation of 480 nm visible light produces the strongest yellow fluorescence. The specific activity reaches 92.67 U g−1, which is higher than that of other graphene quantum dots. The Fe/B-GQD-HSF can catalyze oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine with H2O2 to form blue compound. Based on this, it was used for colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2. The absorbance at 652 nm linearly increases with the increase of H2O2 concentration between 0.5 and 100 μM with detection limit of 0.43 μM. The fluorescence signal linearly decreases with the increase of H2O2 concentration between 0.05 and 100 μM with detection limit of 0.035 μM. The analytical method has been satisfactorily applied in detection of H2O2 in food. The study also paves one way for design and synthesis of functional graphene quantum dots with ideal fluorescence behavior and catalytic activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a reactive oxygen species that is produced in the response to internal and external stressors [1]. The intracellular H2O2 level maintains at an extremely low level under the normal respiratory condition [2]. Excess H2O2 may cause lipid peroxidation, structural damage and function loss of cell membrane, oxidation of important proteins in cellular metabolism and function damages [3]. An obvious change in H2O2 level induces the breakdown of cellular redox equilibrium which may cause intracellularoxidative stressand damage to tissues and organ systems [4]. Food is the main source of H2O2 in human body, because H2O2 is often used to kill harmful microorganisms in production, packaging and storage of food and condiments. In addition, some illegal manufacturers also use excessive H2O2 in food to control mold growth and food bleaching [5]. The consumption of food with excessive H2O2 may induce occurrence of cerebral embolism, gas embolism, gastric distension and other diseases [6]. Development of a rapid and reliable method for detection of H2O2 in food is important to ensure human health.

The analytical methods for H2O2 mainly includes surface-enhanced Raman scattering [7], chemiluminescence [8], fluorescence [9], electrochemical method [10], colorimetric method [11], capillary isochronous electrophoresis [12], high performance liquid chromatography [13], high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [14]. Owing to flexibility, simple operation and high sensitivity, colorimetric and fluorescence methods have received a more attention in H2O2 detection. To improve the sensitivity, horseradish peroxidase [15] was mostly used for colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2. However, its catalytic activity is sensitive to the environmental factors such as temperature, humidity, ionic strength and medium acidity. As a result, environmental factors may cause an obvious change in the enzyme activity and leads to a low reproducibility and even false positive results. To resolve the problem, many nanozymes have been developed for fluorescence and colorimetric detection of H2O2 [16]. The current nanozymes used for detection of H2O2 mainly are noble metal nanocrystal [17], metal oxide [18] and carbon nanomaterial [19]. Due to high peroxidase-like activity and chemical stability, noble metal nanocrystal become the mostly used nanozyme for fluorescence and colorimetric detection of H2O2. Nevertheless, high-cost and resource scarcity restrict its wide use. Although metal oxide can offer both peroxidase-like activity and oxidase-like activity, its peroxidase-like activity is always lower than the oxidase-like activity. The characteristics makes the colorimetric detection of H2O2 shows a relatively high detection limit when metal oxide was used as the nanozyme. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) causes a great interest in colorimetric detection of H2O2, because of good biocompatibility and intrinsic peroxidase-like activity [20].

GQD is a newly emerged carbon nanomaterial with high luminescence efficiency, low toxicity and intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. Different from noble metal and metal oxide, GQD can serve as both fluorescence probe and nanozyme. Hence, it can be directly used for colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2 no need of additional optical probe. However, low fluorescence under visible light excitation and catalytic activity limit the application of GQD in colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2 in biological samples. Several approaches have been developed for improving fluorescence behavior and peroxidase-like activity of GQD. The first approach is to introduce heteroatom into the lattice of graphene sheets for making doped GQD [21]. The introduction of heteroatom changes the electron and energy band structure and leads to an improvement of fluorescence property and catalytic activity. For example, Xu et al. reported one nano space-confined strategy for fabrication of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dot (S-doped GQD) by one-step simple hydrothermal treatment [22]. The resultant S-doped GQD shows good dispersibility, high photostability and abundant functional groups. It was contentedly applied as an optical sensor in direct detection of H2O2. Lin et al. reported synthesis of one nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (N-doped GQD) [23. The result indicates that the introduction of nitrogen enhances the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of GQD [24]. Interestingly, its catalytic activity depends on H2O2 concentration. Based on this, such a N-GQD as fluorescence sensor exhibits sensitive response to H2O2 between 20 and 1170 mu M and glucose between 25 and 375 mu M. The detection limit is up to 5.3 mu M of H2O2 and 16 mu M of glucose [23]. Different from S and N elements, boron is one kind of electron-deficient element. Theoretical investigation reveals that graphitic boron doping in GQD may lead to a red-shift emission of GQD to NIR-MIR region [25]. Unless nonmetal element, metal elements can also be used for fabricating doped GQDs. For example, Li et al. reported synthesis technique for creating Fe-N central single-atom doped graphene quantum dot by solvothermal process. The individual iron atoms form strong bonds with GQD through Fe-N coordination bond [26]. The resultant Fe-doped GQD shows peroxidase-like catalytic activity. It was successfully applied as nanozyme in colorimetric detection of glucose with high sensitivity. Nevertheless, the actual effect of single element doping is very limited to improve the fluorescence behavior and catalytic activity. The second approach is to introduce noble metal nanocrystal to make noble metal nanoparticle- modified GQDs [27]. As noble metal nanocrystal can offer good peroxidase-like activity, its introduction brings an obvious improvement in peroxidase-like activity. However, the combination induces a rapid fluorescence quenching process. As a consequence, the GQD combined by noble metal nanoparticle almost loses the function as fluorescence probe [28]. The third approach is to introduce metal oxide to form metal oxide-GQD nanocomposite [29]. Both GQD and metal oxide are semiconductors, thus their combination may result in an enhanced peroxidase-like activity by engineering band gap and band structure. Meanwhile, the combination significantly improves the oxidase-like activity of metal oxide [30]. High oxidase-like activity promotes oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine in the absence of H2O2. This leads to a significant increase in detection limit of H2O2, and even detection failure. Similar to noble metal nanocrystal, the introduction of metal oxide leads to the loss of functionality of GQD as the fluorescence probe because of its extremely weak fluorescence emission. Despite these advances, design and synthesis of one graphene quantum dot with ideal fluorescence behavior and peroxidase-like activity is highly anticipated.

In the study, we report design and synthesis of histidine, serine and folic acid-functionalized and boron and iron-doped graphene quantum dot (Fe/B-GQD-HSF). The integration of histidine, serine, folic acid with boron achieves to strong fluorescence emission at excitation of visible light. The introduction of iron-doping significantly enhance the peroxidase-like activity. The Fe/B-GQD-HSF exhibits excellent fluorescence behavior and peroxidase-like activity. It was successfully used as optical probe and nanozyme for colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2 in food.

Typically, 3.0 g citric acid, 2.214 g histidine, 0.3 g serine, 0.063 g folic acid, 0.35 g H3BO3 and 0.8 g FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water, transferred into a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave, heated at 180 °C for 4 h, cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 2 min in sequence. The collected supernatant was treated by dialysis with a dialysis bag (MWCO of 3000 Da) and free-drying to obtain solid Fe/B-GQD-HSF. The procedure was also used for synthesis of GQD, GQD-H, GQD-HS, GQD-HSF and B-GQD-HSF.

A mixed solution was prepared by mixing 0.1 mL of 10 mM TMB, 0.1 mL of 10 mM H2O2 and 0.7 mL of the BR buffer solution of pH 3.5. Then, 0.1 mL of the Fe/B-GQD-HSF solution with different concentrations was added. The absorption spectrum and absorbance at 652 nm were measured at different reaction times on the spectrophotometer. The peroxidase-like activity (b, U) was calculated by the equation (1) [31]:
(1)
b =
V

Δ t
where, V, ε, L and ΔA/Δt presents total volume of the reaction system, molar absorption coefficient of TMB (39000 M−1 cm−1), optical length of cell and change rate of the absorbance at 652 nm with reaction time. Further, the specific activity (a, U mg−1) can be calculated by the equation (2) [31]:
(2)
a = b / m
where, m presents the mass (mg) of Fe/B-GQD-HSF in the reaction system.

H2O2 and TMB were used as the substrate for researching steady-state dynamic behavior of the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 under Fe/B-GQD-HSF catalysis, respectively [32]. When H2O2 was used as the substrate, the mixed solution containing 0.1 mL of 10 mM TMB, 0.75 mL of the BR buffer of pH 3.5 and 0.1 mL of the H2O2 solution with a different concentration was employed as the model reaction system. After injected 0.05 mL of 1.0 mg mL−1 Fe/B-GQD-HSF solution, its absorption spectrum and absorbances at 652 nm were monitored by TU-1901 spectrophotometer. When TMB was used as the substrate, another mixed solution containing 0.1 mL of 10 mM H2O2, 0.75 mL of the BR buffer of pH 3.5 and 0.1 mL of the TMB solution with a different concentration was employed as the model reaction system. After, added 0.05 mL of 1.0 mg mL−1 Fe/B-GQD-HSF solution, its absorption spectrum and absorbances at 652 nm were monitored by TU-1901 spectrophotometer. Based on the results of above experimental, the steady-state dynamic parameters of the oxidation of TMB with H2O2 under Fe/B-GQD-HSF catalysis can be calculated using the Lineweaver-Burk equation (1):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The BR buffer of pH 3.5 (0.75 mL) was mixed with 0.1 mL of 10 mM TMB solution and 0.1 mL of the H2O2 standard solution with different concentration or sample solution in a centrifugal tube. Then, added 0.1 mL of 1.0 mg mL−1 Fe/B-GQD-HSF solution and incubated for 5 min. The mixed solution was subject to absorption spectrum scanning, absorbance at 652 nm on TU-1901 spectrophotometer, fluorescence spectrum scanning and fluorescence intensity measuring on the Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence photometer at the excitations of 330 nm ultraviolet light and 480 nm visible light.

Design and synthesis of Fe/B-GQD-HSF was presented at Fig. 1 . Citric acid acts as the carbon source for making graphene sheet. Histidine (His), serine (Ser) and folic acid (FA) act as the function reagents for improving the optical and catalytic behaviors. As these reagents contain electron-donating –OH and –NH- due to their high electronegativity, their introduction enhances the charge density of graphene sheet. This leads to an improved photoluminescence efficiency. In addition, these reagents also contain some special groups and structures with large electron-conjugate system such as carboxyl, imidazolyl and aryl groups. Thus, their introduction expands the electron delocalization range and brings a red shift of fluorescence emission. Different from His, Ser and FA, boric acid (H3BO3) and ferric chloride (FeCl3) were used for introducing heteroatoms of B and Fe in lattice of graphene sheet. B is an electron-deficient element, its introduction greatly speeds up the electron transfer from functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen at the edge of graphene sheets to the center of graphene sheet. This narrows the bandgap and optimizes the band structure, improving the photoluminescence efficiency. As Fe is one kind of transition metal elements with the rich of d-electrons, its introduction not only can accelerate the electron transfer in graphene sheet similar to B but also promote the electron transfer through the conversion between different valent iron species. This accelerates oxidation of TMB with H2O2 and leads to an improved peroxidase-like activity. More importantly, the synergy of histidine, serine, folic acid, boron and iron achieves to significant improvement of optical behavior and peroxidase-like activity to meet the needs of further applications of GQD in fluorescence detection, bioimaging and catalysis. The as-designed Fe/B-GQD-HSF was synthesized by one-step simple hydrothermal treatment. In the synthesis, choosing an appropriate temperature is crucial. The result shows the use of 180 °C can achieve to the above design purpose. At 180 °C, citric acid molecules can be converted to graphene sheet by intermolecular condensation and subsequent carbonization. However, at such a temperature His, Ser FA can remain stable because the temperature is lower than that of their decomposition temperatures. In addition, the temperature of 180 °C can ensure a fast reaction of carboxyl groups at the edge of graphene sheets with amino in the His, Ser FA molecules to from stable amide bond. Further, the mass ratio of histidine, serine, folic acid, boric acid and iron chloride was optimized. An optimized mass ratio of 3: 2.214: 0.3: 0.063: 0.35: 0.8 was obtained to produce the strongest yellow fluorescence.

Further, XPS technique was used as one powerful tool for analyzing the chemical environments of Fe/B-GQD-HSF surface. Fig. 3 shows total XPS spectrum, high-resolution XPS spectra of B1s, C1s, N1s, O1s and Fe2p of Fe/B-GQD-HSF. The total XPS spectrum includes five peaks at 194.8, 284.9, 398.1, 528.9 and 706.9 eV, which can be assigned to B1s, C1s, N1s, O1s and Fe2p. The result demonstrates that Fe/B-GQD-HSF mainly consists of boron (B), carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and iron (Fe) elements. As B element only come from H3BO3 and Fe only come from FeCl3, the above result confirms that B and Fe have successfully doped into the carbon lattice of graphene sheets. The XPS spectrum of B1s includes three peaks at 189.45, 190.28 and 191.06 eV, corresponding to the B-N, B-O and B-C bonds. The XPS spectrum of C1s includes six peaks at 284.8, 285.56, 286.43, 287.58, 289.78 and 299.72 eV, corresponding to the C–B, C–C, C=C, C–O, C–N and COOH bonds. The XPS spectrum of N1s includes three peaks at 396.74, 397.88 and 398.71 eV, corresponding to the pyridinic N, graphitic N and pyrrolic N. The XPS spectrum of O1s includes two peaks at 528.67 and 530.2 eV, corresponding to the C=O and C–O bonds. The XPS spectrum of Fe2p includes four peaks at 708.50, 714.23, 721.15 and 728.25 eV. The two peaks at 721.15 eV and 708.50 eV correspond to Fe2+2p1/2 and Fe2+2p3/2, verifying the presence of Fe2+ specie. The two peaks at 728.25 eV and 714.23 eV correspond to Fe3+2p1/2 and Fe3+2p3/2, verifying the presence of Fe3+ specie [35].

Three-dimensional fluorescence mapping of Fe/B-GQD-HSF was presented at Fig. 4
A. The mapping offers two peaks. One peak is located at fluorescence emission wavelength of420 nm at excitation of 330 nm ultraviolet light and another peak is located at fluorescence emission wavelength of550 nm at excitation of 480 nm visible light. This demonstrates Fe/B-GQD-HSF can produce strong blue fluorescence and strong yellow fluorescence by varying excitation wavelength, indicating that the fluorescence emission depends on the excitation wavelength. Further, Fig. 4B shows the conversion process of blue fluorescence to yellow fluorescence can be divided into three stages. The first stage is one section that the excitation wavelength is between 300 and 355 nm. In the stage, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength is about 420 nm, indicating a blue fluorescence emission. The fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum value at the excitation of 330 nm ultraviolet light and then decreases with increasing excitation wavelength. The second stage is one section that the excitation wavelength is between 370 and 440 nm. In the stage, the maximum emission wavelength is about 470 nm, indicating a blue fluorescence emission. The fluorescence intensity reduces with increasing excitation wavelength. The third is one section that the excitation wavelength is between 440 and 550 nm. In the stage, the maximum emission wavelength is about 550 nm, indicating yellow fluorescence emission. The fluorescence intensity reaches to the maximum when the excitation wavelength is about 480 nm and then rapidly decreases with the increase of excitation wavelength. However, its maximum emission wavelength almost doesn’t depend on the excitation wavelength. The above excitation-dependent behavior is supported by a CIE coordinate analysis in Fig. 4C. This demonstrates that the emitting colors keep as strong blue fluorescence and strong yellow fluorescence in a relatively wide excitation wavelength. To compare the luminescence efficiencies of different graphene quantum dots, the fluorescencequantum yield (FQY) of Fe/B-GQD-HSF was measured at the excitations of ultraviolet light at 330 nm and a visible light at 480 nm by one reported method [36]. The FQY values are 62 % for blue fluorescence emission and 27 % for yellow fluorescence emission. These values are much higher than that of GQD (9 %), verify that Fe/B-GQD-HSF has a good luminescence efficiency at the excitation of both ultraviolet light and visible light.

To test the photostability, the peak fluorescence intensities of Fe/B-GQD-HSF under the excitations of 330 nm ultraviolet light and 480 nm visible light were measured after the Fe/B-GQD-HSF solution of 1.0 mg mL−1 was subjected an ultraviolet lamp radiation (100 W) for different times. Fig. 4D shows the intensities of blue fluorescence and yellow fluorescence can keep almost unchanged within 30 min of the irradiation, showing an excellent photostability. Due to strong yellow fluorescence emission, the Fe/B-GQD-HSF is more suitable as a fluorescence probe incell imaging and biological detection compared with the present blue light graphene quantum dots. This is because the use of low energy visible light as the excitation light can effectively avoid the background fluorescence from protein,nucleic acid and other biologicalmacromolecules under the excitation of ultraviolet light.

The reaction system of H2O2 oxidizing TMB in the presence of catalyst was selected as model reaction to evaluate peroxidase-like activity of Fe/B-GQD-HSF. Fig. 5
A shows the absorption spectra of reaction systems catalyzed by Fe/B-GQD-HSF, B-GQD-HSF, GQD-HSF, GQD-HSF, Fe-GQD and GQD. In the presence of Fe-GQD or GQD at the incubation time of 30 min, no obvious absorption peak appears on the absorption spectrum between 500 and 800 nm, the absorbance at 652 nm is close to zero, indicating an extremely slow reaction process. This confirms that Fe-GQD and GQD only can offer an extremely low peroxidase-like activity. Interestingly, the presence of B-GQD-HSF brings an obvious increase in the absorbance at 652 nm, indicating an improved peroxidase-like activity. Such a high peroxidase-like activity of B-GQD-HSF should be attributed to the introduction of His, Ser, FA and H3BO3. If B-GQD-HSF was replaced by Fe/B-GQD-HSF, the absorbance at 652 nm will significantly increase, showing a much better peroxidase-like activity compared with B-GQD-HSF. This confirms that Fe doping can achieve to an improved peroxidase-like activity.

The reaction kinetic behavior of TMB oxidation catalyzed by Fe/B-GQD-HSF was investigated. For H2O2 substrate, the relationship curves of the reaction rate (V) with H2O2 concentration and reciprocal of H2O2 concentration were shown in Fig. 5B and C. Using the relationship curves for different H2O2 concentrations, the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) and Michaelis-Menten constant (KM) were calculated. The KM and Vmax values are 4.02 μM and 1.73 × 10−7 M s−1, indicating a good affinity of Fe/B-GQD-HSF toward H2O2. For TMB substrate, the relationship curves with TMB concentration and reciprocal of TMB concentration were shown in Fig. 5D and E. Using the relationship curves for different TMB concentrations, the Vmax and KM were calculated. The KM and Vmax values are 260 μM and 2.05 × 10−7 M s−1, indicating a good affinity of Fe/B-GQD-HSF towards TMB.

The enzyme activity of Fe/B-GQD-HSF was measured by varying the amounts of Fe/B-GQD-HSF in the reaction system. Fig. 5F and G present the plot of absorbance at 652 nm with different Fe/B-GQD-HSF dosage vs. the incubation time as well as relationship curve of peroxidase-like activity with Fe/B-GQD-HSF dosage. In all Fe/B-GQD-HSF dosages studied, there is good linear relationship between the absorbance and the incubation time, verifying the oxidation of TMB under Fe/B-GQD-HSF catalysis shows one first-order kinetic behavior. The catalytic activity (b) linearly increases with increasing Fe/B-GQD-HSF dosage (m). The corresponding linear regression is: b (U) = 92.674 × m (mg) − 0.004, with a relative correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.986. The specific activity of Fe/B-GQD-HSF is equal to the slop of the curve from the above equation. The result shows the specific activity is 92.674 U mg−1, which is better than that all graphene quantum dots and the most nanozymes in Table 1 .

To understand the sensing principle, the spectra of Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB+H2O2, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+H2O2, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB, TMB+H2O2, TMB and Fe/B-GQD-HSF reaction systems at the incubation time of 30 min were measured, respectively. Fig. 6
A shows single TMB and single Fe/B-GQD-HSF offer very weak absorbance between 500 and 750 nm. Thus, their existence don’t interfere with colorimetric detection of H2O2. Similar to TMB system and Fe/B-GQD-HSF system, the TMB+H2O2 reaction indicates a very weak absorbance between 500 and 750 nm. This verifies that the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 offers an extremely low reaction rate in the absence of analyst. However, TMB cannot be oxidized by Fe/B-GQD-HSF catalysis in the absence of H2O2. This confirms Fe/B-GQD-HSF has a low oxidase-like activity. Thus, it cannot directly oxidize TMB to form blue product in air. Interestingly, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+H2O2 system shows a relatively high absorption peak. This is because Fe/B-GQD-HSF catalyzes H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical species because of good peroxidase-like activity. The formed radical species may partly oxide Fe/B-GQD-HSF and leads to an increased absorbance. Among the six reaction systems studied, only Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB+H2O2 can offer strong absorption peak at 652 nm. This is attributed to the catalysis of Fe/B-GQD-HSF towards the oxidation of TMB with H2O2. As the absorbance at 652 nm only depend on H2O2 concentration, the above reaction system can be used for the colorimetric detection of H2O2.

To optimize the detection conditions, the effect of Fe/B-GQD-HSF concentration, medium pH and incubation time on the absorbance at 652 nm were researched, respectively. Fig. 6B shows with the increase of Fe/B-GQD-HSF concentration the absorbance rapidly increases when the concentration is lower than 0.1 mg mL−1. A low Fe/B-GQD-HSF concentration leads to a slow oxidation rate of TMB with H2O2. Du to less blue products generated within the incubation time of 30 min, the system indicates a low absorbance at 652 nm. With the increase of Fe/B-GQD-HSF the oxidation of TMB is accelerated and results in a rapid increase in the absorbance at 652 nm because of the formation of more blue products within the incubation time of 30 min. However, the absorbance tends to stable when the Fe/B-GQD-HSF concentration is more than 0.1 mg mL−1. To save the catalyst and obtain a high sensitivity, the Fe/B-GQD-HSF concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1 was selected to colorimetric detection of H2O2. Fig. 6C presents the plot of absorbance at 652 nm vs. the medium pH value. When the pH value is in the range of 2–3.5, the absorbance remains almost unchanged, indicating a good stability. The absorbance will rapidly reduce with increasing pH value when the pH value is in the range of 4–6. This reduce should be attributed to the deprotonation of TMB and Fe/B-GQD-HSF. However, such a rapid change on the absorbance with pH value can be used for sensitively colorimetric detection of pH value. To obtain a high sensitivity, the pH value of 3.5 was used for colorimetric detection of H2O2. Fig. 6D presents the plot of absorbance at 652 nm vs. the incubation time. With the increase of incubation time, the absorbance will significantly increase when the time is less than 10 min. This is because a more time forms more blue products caused by the oxidation of TMB with H2O2. This leads to an increased absorbance. However, the change tends to stable when the time is more than 10 min. This is because the oxidation of TMB is close to the chemical equilibrium, showing a relatively stable absorbance. To save time and obtain high sensitivity, the incubation time of 30 min was selected for colorimetric detection of H2O2.

To test the possibility of colorimetric detection of H2O2, the spectra in the presence of different concentration of H2O2 were measured. Fig. 6E shows that the increase of H2O2 concentration causes a rapid increase in the absorbance. This is because a higher concentration of H2O2 leads to the oxidation of more TMB molecules catalyzed by Fe/B-GQD-HSF. The formation of more blue products brings an increased absorbance. Further, Fig. 6F shows there is a good linear relationship between absorbance at 652 nm and H2O2 concentration in the range of 0.5–100 μM. The linear regression equation is: A652nm = 0.0201 × CH2O2 + 0.1097 with R2 of 0.997. The detection limit was found to be 0.43 μM.

To understand the sensing principle, the fluorescence spectra of TMB, Fe/B-GQD-HSF, TMB+H2O2, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+H2O2, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB and Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB+H2O2 reaction systems under the excitations of 330 nm ultraviolet light and 480 nm visible light were measured. Fig. 7
A and B shows that both TMB and TMB+H2O2 systems can produce strong blue fluorescence at the excitation of 330 nm ultraviolet light, verifying TMB can offer a high luminescence efficiency under the excitation of 330 nm ultraviolet light. Compared with TMB, the TMB+H2O2 system shows a slightly stronger fluorescence emission. This is because high electron density of H2O2 molecule. TMB can interact with H2O2 and causes a rapid energy transfer process from H2O2 to TMB. This leads to an improved luminescence efficiency. However, no fluorescence peak appears on the fluorescence spectra of TMB and TMB+H2O2 reaction systems at the excitation of 480 nm visible light. This demonstrates that TMB cannot produce strong fluorescence emission at the excitation of visible light. Different from TMB, Fe/B-GQD-HSF exhibits one strong blue fluorescence at the excitation of 330 nm ultraviolet light and one strong yellow fluorescence at the excitation of 480 nm visible light. Among all fluorescence spectra at the excitation of 480 nm visible light, the Fe/B-GQD-HSF shows the strongest yellow fluorescence. Interestingly, the combination of Fe/B-GQD-HSF with TMB brings an obvious reduce in the fluorescence intensity at the excitations of both 330 nm ultraviolet light and 480 nm visible light. This confirms the combination causes a rapid energy transfer between TMB and Fe/B-GQD-HSF. This leads to a reduced fluorescence intensity of Fe/B-GQD-HSF and TMB. Similar to TMB, the introduction of H2O2 brings a reduce in the fluorescence intensity at the excitations of both 330 nm ultraviolet light and 480 nm visible light, especially the yellow fluorescence at the excitation of 480 nm visible light. Compared with Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB system, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB+H2O2 system shows a significantly reduced blue fluorescence and yellow fluorescence, showing an obvious fluorescence quenching process. For the blue fluorescence in Fig. 7A, the blue fluorescence quenching should be mainly attributed to the reduce of TMB, because H2O2 only can cause a slight change in the blue fluorescence intensity. With the oxidation of TMB, the concentration of TMB in the reaction system rapidly decreases and leads to an obvious blue fluorescence quenching of TMB. For the yellow fluorescence in Fig. 7B, the yellow fluorescence quenching should be attributed to Fe/B-GQD-HSF, because TMB cannot produce strong yellow fluorescence emission. Based on the above analysis, we can draw one conclusion: The fluorescence quenching only depends on the oxidation of TMB catalyzed by Fe/B-GQD-HSF. Therefore, Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB+H2O2 system can be used for fluorescence detection of H2O2. In the detection, both blue fluorescence of TMB and yellow fluorescence of Fe/B-GQD-HSF can be used as the signal for detection of H2O2. To obtain a high sensitivity, their combination was employed as the signal for fluorescence detection of H2O2.

Fig. 7C and D present the fluorescence spectra of Fe/B-GQD-HSF+TMB+H2O2 reaction system at the excitations of 330 nm ultraviolet light and 480 nm visible light in the presence of different H2O2 concentrations. The increase of H2O2 brings a rapid reduce in the blue fluorescence and yellow fluorescence. A high concentration of H2O2 causes a faster oxidation of TMB. This leads to more reduction of TMB and H2O2 in the system. The former produce more blue fluorescence quenching of TMB (F330) and the latter produce more yellow fluorescence quenching of Fe/B-GQD-HSF (F480). Further, Fig. 7E shows the relationship curve of F330/F480 value with the H2O2 concentration. The F330/F480 value will linearly decrease with increasing H2O2 concentration in the range of 0.05–100 μM. The linear equation is: the F330/F480 = -0.0677 × CH2O2 (μM) + 11.14 with R2 of 0.992 with a detection limit of 0.035 μM. For comparison, the analytical parameters of reported methods for detection of H2O2 was listed in Table 2 . Table 2 shows the proposed method offers a better sensitivity compared with other methods.

To test the selectivity, effect of the interference species that may exist in biological samples on the fluorescence signal were investigated. Fig. 7F shows that 1 mg of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, glucose, sucrose, phenylalanine (Phe), histidine (His), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly) and urea only can cause a change of less than 5 % in the peak fluorescence intensity, indicating a good selectivity.

Bamboo bean, flour, milk, juice and beer were purchased from the local supermarket in Wuxi city. Before pretreated, bamboo bean was cut into small pieces (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm) and then grinded into fine power by family-use grinder. For bamboo bean, flour and milk, 0.5 g of sample was dispersed in 10 mL of 1.0 wt% trichloroacetic acid aqueous solution [51]. Followed by vortexing for 20 min, centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min, adjusting pH to 7.0 and centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The collected supernatant was used for the fluorescence detection of H2O2. For juice and beer samples, sample was diluted with the PBS of pH 7.4 with a volume ratio of 1:9 and then used for the fluorescence detection of H2O2 [52]. The spiked recovery test was completed by adding H2O2 standard solution into sample solution. Table 3 shows the H2O2 recovery is in the range of 98.0 to 104.0 %, verifying good accuracy and precision.

The study demonstrated design and synthesis of histidine, serine and folic acid-functionalized and boron and iron-doped graphene quantum dot (Fe/B-GQD-HSF). Synergy of histidine, serine, folic acid, boron and iron achieves to unique fluorescence behavior and high peroxidase-like activity. Ultraviolet light excitation of 330 nm can produce one strong blue fluorescence and visible light excitation of 480 nm can produce one strong yellow fluorescence. The peroxidase-like activity reaches to 92.67 U g−1, which is more than that of all reported graphene quantum dots and most metal-based nanozymes. Fe/B-GQD-HSF can catalyze oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to form blue product. The oxidation causes a sensitive change in absorbance at 652 nm due to the formation of blue product as well as blue fluorescence quenching of TMB due to the reduce of TMB and yellow fluorescence quenching of Fe/B-GQD-HSF due to the reduce of H2O2. The analytical method based on Fe/B-GQD-HSF has been successfully applied in colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2O2 in food. More importantly, the study offers one way for design and synthesis of functional graphene quantum dots with excellent optical properties and ultrahigh catalytic activity.
